questions.prompt: A team prototypes a 4-agent system (planner, researcher, writer, editor) for content generation. Cost is $0.40/run, latency 35s. A single agent with the same tools costs $0.10/run, 12s latency, and matches quality on evals. Right move?

choices.text: Add a fifth agent for editing.

Ship the single-agent version. Multi-agent was complexity for its own sake — same quality at 4× the cost and 3× the latency. Reserve multi-agent for cases where the work cleanly splits into roles with different tools/models/safety profiles, or where tool overload (15+) forces a split.

questions.explanation: Multi-agent earns complexity when single-agent is genuinely cracking. When a single-agent matches quality at a fraction of the cost, the multi-agent design was vanity, not value. Default single-agent; promote when there's a clear concrete reason.

questions.prompt: When is multi-agent the right call?

choices.text: Whenever you have multiple LLM calls.

When roles cleanly differ (different prompts, tools, models, or safety profiles); when a single agent has too many tools to reason about (15+); when logical workstreams can run in parallel; when you need to tightly scope an agent's safety surface. Otherwise, single-agent + workflow scaffolding around it.

questions.explanation: Clear role differentiation is the main signal. A 'reader' agent with read-only tools + a 'writer' agent with approval gates makes sense. A 'planner' that just hands off to a 'executor' without any role difference is overhead. Test multi-agent design by writing the agent diagram — if it tangles, the design is wrong.
